addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
`tmux attach -t skirmish`

If you're already inside a tmux session, use `tmux switch-client -t skirmish` instead.

message.content: I downloaded an app for my mac called I think 'the windows app' that is supposed to enable me to do remote desktop via my tailscale.  Unfortunately, it does not seem to work.  Websearch, and talk me through setup of my windows computers- maybe I have to enable something on them?

content.text: I'll research the current state of Microsoft's "Windows App" and Tailscale RDP setup, then walk you through it.

input.query: Microsoft Windows App Mac remote desktop setup enable Remote Desktop on Windows PC
Tailscale Windows remote desktop RDP setup connect Mac Windows App

content.content:
Web search results for query: "Microsoft Windows App Mac remote desktop setup enable Remote Desktop on Windows PC"

Links: [{"title":"Connect to Windows devices and apps with Windows App - Windows App | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-app/get-started-connect-devices-desktops-apps"},{"title":"Connect to Remote Desktop Services and remote PCs on macOS - Remote Desktop client | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/remote-desktop-client/remote-desktop-macos"},{"title":"How to Use Microsoft Remote Desktop on Mac [Full Guide]","url":"https://deskin.io/resource/blog/how-to-use-microsoft-remote-desktop-mac"},{"title":"Setting up Microsoft Remote Desktop for Mac to access Windows PC","url":"https://www.helpwire.app/blog/how-to-setup-remote-desktop-connection-for-mac/"},{"title":"How to Access Microsoft Remote Desktop on your Mac","url":"https://www.techrepublic.com/article/pro-tip-remote-desktop-on-mac-what-you-need-to-know/"},{"title":"Article - Desktop: Windows to Mac Setup","url":"https://help.nmsu.edu/TDClient/267/Portal/KB/ArticleDet?ID=4753"},{"title":"How to Use Microsoft Remote Desktop Connection for Mac","url":"https://www.anyviewer.com/how-to/microsoft-remote-desktop-connection-for-mac-2578.html"},{"title":"macOS: Connect to a Remote Windows Computer | IT@UMN | The people behind the technology","url":"https://it.umn.edu/services-technologies/how-tos/macos-connect-remote-windows-computer"},{"title":"3 Ways to Remote Into PC from Mac Smoothly [6 Recommended Tools]","url":"https://deskin.io/resource/blog/remote-into-pc-from-mac"},{"title":"How to use Remote Desktop - Microsoft Support","url":"https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/how-to-use-remote-desktop-5fe128d5-8fb1-7a23-3b8a-41e636865e8c"}]

Here are the key results for setting up Microsoft Windows App on Mac for remote desktop access:

## Overview

To connect to a remote PC on macOS, download and install Windows App from the Mac App Store. The Remote Desktop client for macOS has been replaced by Windows App.

## Enable Remote Desktop on Windows PC

Before connecting from your Mac, you must first enable Remote Desktop on your Windows PC:

Go to Settings > System > Remote Desktop and toggle the switch to enable remote connections. Important note: Remote Desktop is only compatible with Windows Professional editions—if you're using the Home edition, you'll need to upgrade to Pro before you can use the service.

## Connect from Mac to Windows PC

Select the plus (+) icon, then select Add PC. In the dialog box that appears, for PC Name, enter the hostname or IP address of the remote PC you want to connect to.

You can make other changes, such as add a user account to connect with, provide a friendly name for the connection, configure display settings, and configure device and audio redirection.

The search results include information from official Microsoft documentation and comprehensive setup guides for connecting your Mac to a Windows PC using Windows App.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Access remote desktops using Windows RDP · Tailscale Docs","url":"https://tailscale.com/docs/solutions/access-remote-desktops-using-windows-rdp"},{"title":"Remote Desktop Perfected: How Tailscale + RDP Creates the Ultimate Secure Remote Access Solution | by 404: Office Not Found | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@404officenotfound/remote-desktop-perfected-how-tailscale-rdp-creates-the-ultimate-secure-remote-access-solution-c59c793c8c75"},{"title":"Tailscale-RDP-Setup-From-Mac-to-Windows/README.md at main · Mario-CyberS/Tailscale-RDP-Setup-From-Mac-to-Windows","url":"https://github.com/Mario-CyberS/Tailscale-RDP-Setup-From-Mac-to-Windows/blob/main/README.md"},{"title":"How to install & Configure Tailscale in Linux, Windows & MacOS? - DevSecOps School","url":"https://devsecopsschool.com/blog/how-to-install-configure-tailscale-in-linux-windows-macos/"},{"title":"GitHub - Mario-CyberS/Tailscale-RDP-Setup-From-Mac-to-Windows: Here we will Set-up TailScale (a mesh VPN service that creates a secure, private network between devices using WireGuard) to allow us connection from my Macbook pro to my Windows 11 PC. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Mario-CyberS/Tailscale-RDP-Setup-From-Mac-to-Windows"}]

Based on the search results, here's information about setting up Tailscale for Windows RDP access, including connecting from Mac:

## Basic Setup Overview

To connect to a remote Windows PC with RDP using Tailscale, you need the Tailscale client installed on at least two devices: the remote Windows PC you want to access and the device you'll connect from.

## Installation Steps

1. **Install Tailscale**: Download the Tailscale client for your device and install it. When you launch the app, log in using an existing identity provider such as Apple, Google, or Microsoft.

2. **Configure the Remote Windows PC**: You'll configure Windows RDP on the remote Windows PC. The instructions for configuring Windows RDP vary between Windows distributions, with specific setup for Windows 11 Pro.

## Connecting from Mac

Install Tailscale on whatever device you want to connect from — Mac, Linux, Windows, Android, iOS, or even Chromebook. Sign in with the same account you used on the host machine.

For the computer name, enter your Windows host's Tailscale machine name (e.g., "my-desktop") or its Tailscale IP address (starts with 100.x.x.x).

## Using Microsoft Remote Desktop on Mac

To connect from Mac specifically, you can use the Microsoft Remote Desktop application. It works seamlessly whether you're on your home network, coffee shop WiFi, or cellular data halfway around the world.

## Security Benefits

Access a Windows PC securely from anywhere using Remote Desktop and Tailscale. Connect devices on your private tailnet without port forwarding or public IPs.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

lastPrompt: I downloaded an app for my mac called I think 'the windows app' that is supposed to enable me to do remote desktop via my tailscale.  Unfortunately, it does not seem to work.  Websearch, and talk me t…

content.text:
Good — I've got the current picture. Here's what's going on and how to fix it.

First, the naming confusion: **"Windows App"** is Microsoft's new name for what used to be "Microsoft Remote Desktop." It's the correct app — it's the *client* on your Mac. The reason it's "not working" is almost always that the **Windows machine isn't set up to accept the connection yet**. The client is fine; the host needs configuring.

There are three things that all have to be true. Let's check them in order.

## 1. Your Windows PC must be Windows **Pro** (not Home)

This is the #1 silent failure. **Windows Home edition cannot host Remote Desktop at all** — the setting simply isn't there. Only Pro, Enterprise, or Education can be an RDP *host*.

Check on each Windows PC:
- Press `Win + R`, type `winver`, hit Enter.
- It'll say "Windows 11 Pro" or "Windows 11 Home" etc.

If it says **Home**, that's your blocker. Options: upgrade to Pro (Settings → System → Activation, ~$99), or use a different tool (Tailscale's own guide and many people use [RustDesk](https://rustdesk.com) or Chrome Remote Desktop on Home editions).

## 2. Enable Remote Desktop on the Windows PC

On each Pro machine:
- **Settings → System → Remote Desktop** → toggle **Remote Desktop** to **On**. Confirm the prompt.
- Note the **PC name** shown right there (e.g. `DESKTOP-ABC123`) — though for Tailscale you'll use the Tailscale name/IP instead.
- Click into the settings and make sure **"Keep my PC awake for connections when it is plugged in"** is handled — a sleeping PC won't answer.

Also confirm your login user is allowed: by default the account you're logged in as can connect. If you use a **Microsoft account** to sign into Windows, your username for RDP is often your email — but the more reliable path is to add/confirm a local-style entry under "Remote Desktop users."

## 3. Tailscale must be running on **both** ends, and you must use the Tailscale address

This is the second most common issue. Tailscale only routes traffic while the app is **running and logged in** on both devices.

- On the Windows PC: open Tailscale, confirm it's logged in and shows "Connected." Note its **Tailscale IP** (starts with `100.x.x.x`) or its **MagicDNS machine name** (e.g. `my-desktop`).
- On your Mac: same — Tailscale running, logged into the **same account/tailnet**.
- Quick proof they can see each other: on your Mac, open Terminal and run `ping 100.x.x.x` (the Windows Tailscale IP). If pings come back, the network is good and the problem is purely RDP-side. If they don't, it's a Tailscale problem, not an RDP one.

Then in **Windows App** on your Mac:
- Click **+** → **Add PC**.
- **PC name:** enter the **Tailscale IP** (`100.x.x.x`) — I'd start with the raw IP, not the name, to rule out DNS issues.
- **User account:** add your Windows username + password (so it doesn't re-prompt).
- Save, then double-click to connect.

---

### Fastest way to diagnose which of the three is failing

1. `winver` on Windows → rules in/out the **Home edition** blocker.
2. From Mac Terminal, `ping <windows-tailscale-IP>` → rules in/out the **Tailscale** layer.
3. If ping works but RDP fails → it's the **Remote Desktop toggle / user permissions** on Windows.

If you tell me what `winver` says on your Windows boxes and whether the ping comes back, I can pinpoint the exact fix. One more useful detail: a Windows PC that's **asleep** won't accept connections, so for headless/remote use you'll want to set it to never sleep while plugged in.

Sources:
- [Connect to Windows devices and apps with Windows App — Microsoft Learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-app/get-started-connect-devices-desktops-apps)
- [How to use Remote Desktop — Microsoft Support](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/how-to-use-remote-desktop-5fe128d5-8fb1-7a23-3b8a-41e636865e8c)
- [Access remote desktops using Windows RDP — Tailscale Docs](https://tailscale.com/docs/solutions/access-remote-desktops-using-windows-rdp)
- [Tailscale RDP Setup From Mac to Windows — GitHub](https://github.com/Mario-CyberS/Tailscale-RDP-Setup-From-Mac-to-Windows)

content: Goal: remote desktop into your Windows PCs from your Mac over Tailscale via the Windows App. Next: on each Windows PC run winver to confirm it's Pro (not Home), then enable Settings, System, Remote Desktop. (disable recaps in /config)
